posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked:
People often remark that Jadot made great wines in ‘97. And that Truchot made great ‘04s. Beyond Burgundy, ‘08 Rayas way out-performs its vintage; so does ‘02 Monfortino; and so does ‘11 Ridge Monte Bello.

What are some other examples?

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Although I have not had it, and therefore cannot verify the claim, people speak highly of Chave Hermitage 2003. It could therefore make the list of great wines in absolutely stunningly awful vintages.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Jadot 1997
Truchot 2003
I also thought about Ridge Monte Bello 2011

posts.name: Andrew Northup
posts.cooked: Timely post as I’m looking at some 87 Produttori right now…would love thoughts

posts.name: Ron Erickson
posts.cooked: 1994 Chateau Pontet Canet

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked:
I’ve also always heard that Roumier way outperformed in ‘95, but at least as far as I can recall, I haven’t had any of the wines.

‘10 Vilmart Coeur de Cuvée is pretty phenomenal for a reputedly weak vintage, but I haven’t had enough other ‘10 Champagnes to compare.

posts.name: Br1an Th0rne
posts.cooked:
1994 Chateau Pontet Canet

For my tastes (much more on the classic end of the spectrum) I believe many ‘94 Bordeaux have turned out great, given the reputation of the vintage. I have great experiences with the 94 Pontet Canet as well (actually bought a case of magnums late last year at auction after drinking almost all of my original stash) and the 94 Leoville Barton might be even better. 94 Latour has been awesome as well, but for the price it had better be!

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked: 1998 Araujo!!!

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
95 Roumier wines are better than 96.
1980 Ponsot CdlR was great.
1997 Faiveley wines were great.
Probably hundreds of examples of wines rising above vintage and appellation.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
Although I have not had it, and therefore cannot verify the claim, people speak highly of Chave Hermitage 2003. It could therefore make the list of great wines in absolutely stunningly awful vintages.

IMO that wine is horrific, the poster child of a too hot year. But Chave indeed excels in poor years on the lean side, for example 1987, 1992, and 2002. Those wines don’t stand out young but they age beautifully.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: '99 Palmer

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
1994 Chateau Pontet Canet

For my tastes (much more on the classic end of the spectrum) I believe many ‘94 Bordeaux have turned out great, given the reputation of the vintage. I have great experiences with the 94 Pontet Canet as well (actually bought a case of magnums late last year at auction after drinking almost all of my original stash) and the 94 Leoville Barton might be even better. 94 Latour has been awesome as well, but for the price it had better be!

I like 1994 Bordeaux though I don’t think they will appeal to everyone. As you say, it’s for those who like classic Bordeaux (if you like 95, 88, 81, and 75 you may well like 94 as well, though it’s not quite like any of them). Some of the prices on WS are a bit scary, though. Of the wines I own, the best (and probably most friendly to many people) are Angelus and Haut Brion. Both are excellent but I couldn’t recommend them at current WS low.

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng

2002 Chave Hermitage is really good

2010 Pierre Peters Chetillons is delicious but I don’t know that 2010 is a consensus “bad” vintage, unlike say 2011

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
Craig, I thought (and feared) that might be the case - i.e. I would “enjoy” the wine about as much as you and about as much as all of the other '03 Hermitage I’ve tried. Oh well.

For my second nomination: Sauzet Puligny Montrachet Combettes 2006, maybe I’ve just been lucky, but outstanding wine compared to my other '06 experiences. Better than the Batard.

I would 2nd 1999 Palmer, except I’m unsure the vintage qualifies as mediocre.

posts.name: Ian Stigoman
posts.cooked: Excellent producers produce excellent product regardless of the vintage. If you’re buying to drink instead of collect or speculate, the lower tier vintages are sometimes tastier and more accessible than the great vintages, and are certainly less expensive on the secondary market.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Margaux appellation 1979. Pomerol did pretty well too.

Is 1979 a mediocre vintage? It was the first vintage I bought when I got to DC out of law school and, while it is not a great vintage, I have had a bunch of really good ones including Palmer, Lafleur, and Pichon Lalande. I think of it as a very good vintage, probably the second or third best of the 1970s (certainly behind 1970 and around the same as 1978), not a mediocre one.

Mark, I know you drink a lot more Bordeaux than I do, and I am sure you have a lot more experience with 1979s than I do (mine were drunk some time ago) and I recognize we are now talking about an era with a bunch of underperforming estates, and so I am curious about your thoughts on the vintage.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:

2011 Vieux Chateau Certan

All outstanding. Had that Latour last year.

A great QPR is 1983 Cantemerle.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Margaux appellation 1979. Pomerol did pretty well too.

Is 1979 a mediocre vintage? It was the first vintage I bought when I got to DC out of law school and, while it is not a great vintage, I have had a bunch of really good ones including Palmer, Lafleur, and Pichon Lalande. I think of it as a very good vintage, probably the second or third best of the 1970s (certainly behind 1970 and around the same as 1978), not a mediocre one.

Mark, I know you drink a lot more Bordeaux than I do, and I am sure you have a lot more experience with 1979s than I do (mine were drunk some time ago) and I recognize we are now talking about an era with a bunch of underperforming estates, and so I am curious about your thoughts on the vintage.

This is an ongoing debate here. I’m a 79, so have had a few and I think it’s a decent but not great vintage, while other people think it’s better than that, so views vary.

Personally, my vote is for the 1992 Bonneau Cuvee Celestins. I don’t even like Grenache very much, but that’s a magical bottle of wine.
Too many shitty 2007 Oregon Pinot Noirs to mention…

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
… Chave indeed excels in poor years on the lean side, for example 1987, 1992, and 2002. Those wines don’t stand out young but they age beautifully.

The 87 & 92 were examples I was going to use. Not ‘great’, but better than any others (for the vintage) and quite graceful.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
‘10 Vilmart Coeur de Cuvée is pretty phenomenal for a reputedly weak vintage

I love the 2010. Maybe the vintage wasn’t bad, but it’s certainly not thought to be great. I haven’t tasted many yet myself. It’s definitely a pattern for Vilmart, though. I thought the 2007 (again, not a bad vintage, but not great) was incredible, and the 2001 (truly a bad vintage), which I had several times, is one of the best Champagnes I’ve ever had.

Quite a few 1998 Napa Cabs have turned out very well, but maybe the problem was the critics not understanding the vintage when it was released and a lot of people believing them, rather than it actually not being a good vintage.

2003s from Willi Schaefer and some other producers in the Mosel. Like the '98 Napa Cabs, these have taken some time to come around, but the Schaefer Spatlesen in particular are great wines, not just in the context of the vintage.
03 and 80 DRC’s

And, I agree on DRC in 1980. I bought the 1980 Grands Echezeaux when it was first released for $35 and it was fabulous.

posts.name: Doug Lee

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Are folks distinguishing here between merely good and truly mediocre vintages? And are they viewing the vintage through the past historical lens or right now? For example, I wouldn’t put 2000 red Burgundy in the mediocre camp even if they were thought of that way in the past. 2000 red Burg is much better than mediocre and that’s been known for a number of years now. Similarly a number of 1994 Bordeaux I’ve had over the last couple years has me rethinking whether there are a number of good wines in a mediocre vintage or just a vintage that took a lot of time and should now be understood as a good vintage, albeit with characteristics that not all enjoy.

Anyway, I’ll throw my cap in with the 1981 La Tache a number of us shared in 2018. That was a ridiculously good bottle of wine.

posts.name: Vinod Stalam
posts.cooked:
Fourriers 2011s
1998 Abreu Madrona Ranch
Selosse and Bouchard in “off vintages”
Pegau 2013

As I go deeper down the rabbit hole, I’m getting closer to producer preference over vintage almost every time.

Good to see the Ridge and VCC 2011s on people’s lists, may need to check in on those.

Similarly a number of 1994 Bordeaux I’ve had over the last couple years has me rethinking whether there are a number of good wines in a mediocre vintage or just a vintage that took a lot of time and should now be understood as a good vintage, albeit with characteristics that not all enjoy.

Anyway, I’ll throw my cap in with the 1981 La Tache a number of us shared in 2018. That was a ridiculously good bottle of wine.

I really was thinking about what is a mediocre vintage even in the context of the 2011 Ridge Monte Bello I mentioned. Probably a mediocre vintage in Napa (let us give it a few more years first) but is it in the Santa Cruz Mountains?

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Margaux appellation 1979. Pomerol did pretty well too.

Is 1979 a mediocre vintage? It was the first vintage I bought when I got to DC out of law school and, while it is not a great vintage, I have had a bunch of really good ones including Palmer, Lafleur, and Pichon Lalande. I think of it as a very good vintage, probably the second or third best of the 1970s (certainly behind 1970 and around the same as 1978), not a mediocre one.

Mark, I know you drink a lot more Bordeaux than I do, and I am sure you have a lot more experience with 1979s than I do (mine were drunk some time ago) and I recognize we are now talking about an era with a bunch of underperforming estates, and so I am curious about your thoughts on the vintage.

This is an ongoing debate here. I’m a 79, so have had a few and I think it’s a decent but not great vintage, while other people think it’s better than that, so views vary.

Personally, my vote is for the 1992 Bonneau Cuvee Celestins. I don’t even like Grenache very much, but that’s a magical bottle of wine.

My Eureka wine was a 1979, the Giscours, so I am a little biased. Tasted a few months ago, it is still vibrant and wonderful.

But overall it is a solid vintage, although for the most part, it is beginning to fade. The exceptions were the two appellations I mentioned, Pomerol and Margaux, and a few individual chateaux, such as Montrose and Haut Brion.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Although I have not had it, and therefore cannot verify the claim, people speak highly of Chave Hermitage 2003. It could therefore make the list of great wines in absolutely stunningly awful vintages.

It is questionable what is classified as “mediocre” … 1991 Bordeaux is, 2002 Chateauneuf-dP is, too imho …

Northern Rhone/Hermitage 2003 is certainly NOT mediocre … it is an extreme vintage, with overripeness and low acidity, but huge concentration …
someone may hate it, some may love it … but the definition “mediocre” is completely off … and “awful” might be an expression of personal distaste, but is certainly not true for many wines …

Chave Hermitage 2003 is such an extreme wine, Jean-Louis himself is unsure about it, but I assured him that I believe in this wine, and that it will turn out great eventually, when fruit and structure find a certain balance, but rather in 2030-2050 than next year …
There might still be some who hate it … they shouldn´t buy … or sell …

I could name a lot of successes imho - also in the South and in Burgundy, but under these certain aspects …

(e.g. Rostaing Cote blonde & LL, Pegau Da Capo … Liger-Belair all cuvees …)

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
I’ve had the 80 PLL three times since 2016, one bottle was good, two were terrific. FWIW, here’s my notes on the two good bottles:

1980 Château Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande, 4/30/2017, 93 points
Another terrific bottle of this, right in my stylistic sweet spot. Smells of wintergreen, juniper, dark chocolate, and rocks. Might be too green for some, but this has plenty of fruit and concentration. Fully resolved, at point.

1980 Château Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande, 5/13/2016, 93 points
Served double blind, we quickly zeroed in on 1980s cab-based Bordeaux. Beautiful old-school bouquet here, loaded with roasted poblano pepper, wintergreen, leafy notes, mint and some cedar. The palate was round and smooth, with terrific fruit and acids. I thought this was in a great place, can’t get any better than now. Someone commented this smelled like the 85 Beychevelle we’d had recently, and that seemed about right. Was surprised on the reveal, didn’t know the 1980 vintage could be this good (though PLL was pretty bullet-proof in that era).

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
The 1980 Pichon Lalande and even more so the 1989 Montelena Estate cab. Birthyear of a wine buddy, so we’ve given 1980 a repeatedly look and these two wines are outstanding in a universally panned vintage.

89 Montelena was tannic when young but did come around.

I tried to type 1980 for both wines, by it came out 1989 on my phone’s tiny keyboard. I edited my post, it’s the 1980 Montelena that has really surprised and impressed, again and again.

That said, 1989 is a very underrated vintage in Napa in my experience (in particular the 89 Ridge Monte Bello is sensational), though I haven’t had the Montelena.

1980 Château Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande, 4/30/2017, 93 points
Another terrific bottle of this, right in my stylistic sweet spot. Smells of wintergreen, juniper, dark chocolate, and rocks. Might be too green for some, but this has plenty of fruit and concentration. Fully resolved, at point.

1980 Château Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande, 5/13/2016, 93 points
Served double blind, we quickly zeroed in on 1980s cab-based Bordeaux. Beautiful old-school bouquet here, loaded with roasted poblano pepper, wintergreen, leafy notes, mint and some cedar. The palate was round and smooth, with terrific fruit and acids. I thought this was in a great place, can’t get any better than now. Someone commented this smelled like the 85 Beychevelle we’d had recently, and that seemed about right. Was surprised on the reveal, didn’t know the 1980 vintage could be this good (though PLL was pretty bullet-proof in that era).

Thanks for the notes, Pat.

In those days, PL was only about 45% cabernet and 35% merlot, plus cab franc and petite verdot – by far the lowest portion of cabernet of any major Pauillac, I believe. They’ve upped it to more than 60% cab now.

1973, 1974, 1975, 1977, 1981, 1982 (these were ok), 1983 (very inconsistent), 1984, 1994, 1997, 2004, 2011 in Burgundy

And I am being generous to these regions.

Thanks Howard. For the compliment and your list of mediocre vintages
I agree the weather conditions were poor these growing seasons. But still you can find interesting wines. Albeit some are oth soon

Ed_Steinway:

I would also add the 1998 Shafer Hillside Select. Every bottle that we have had has been outstanding.

That was the first bottle that came to my mind.

Forman also did well in poor/lesser vintages. The ‘93 has always been a lovely wine.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: 94 Raveneau Valmur. About $30/bottle. Wish I had a bottle still.

1997 is no way an off vintage in Burgundy, it s also not great but some great wines and many very good are to be found, only as few examples: Vosne Reignots Bouchard P&F, RSV Hudelot-N, Cathiard, Arnoux and Confuron, Echezeaux Rouget -
quite the same for 2007
Maybe people call all vintages that are not great through out “off” ???

posts.display_username: John B a s k a m

posts.cooked:
Bweiss:

2020 Walter Scott Bois Mois Chardonnay (a screaming value if you can find any)

But I don’t think 2020 qualifies as a mediocre vintage. It was shaping up to be an excellent vintage from a weather standpoint prior to the wildfires and any reputable producer who was able to produce non-smoke tainted wines made great stuff (ex: Goodfellow whites, the PGC Reserve).

M

1972, 1973, 1974, 1977, 1980, 1984, 1987, 1991, 1992 and 2013 in Bordeaux (plus a good chunk of the 1960s)
1973, 1974, 1975, 1977, 1981, 1982 (these were ok), 1983 (very inconsistent), 1984, 1994, 1997, 2004, 2011 in Burgundy

And I am being generous to these regions.

I would only have an issue with 1980 and 2013 in BDX and 1997 and 2011 in Burgundy, rest are full on buyer beware.

What do you like in 2013? I bought nothing from that vintage, even passed in a 1/2 price offer on Ausone. Prolly should have taken that one. I tend to prefer Ausone in years like 08 and 11 over the monster of vintages like 15.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Kris_Patten:

I think you’d like Carmes Haut Brion, I liked PC too, probably not your wheelhouse, can’t recall a couple St. Estephe and Graves that were good too. Whites were excellent as were a couple Sauternes.

Les Carmes is an interesting call-out, since in 2013 they went quite heavy with Cab Franc, 59%. Just checked Leve’s site on that. I skipped this vintage as a whole, but also note that this Chateau was going through major chance from 2010 through 2014. Would love to try it, but seems hard to find, and now all wines from this estate have gotten pricey. I recall after the sale and conversion, since I had really loved the old version, I went on a little buying kick to backfill. Was grabbing 2010s for $55 from Total Wine in that 2017-18 timeframe, and found some 2005 for not much more. My yummy 2000s are all gone.

1998 Robert Mondavi Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon. (In my experience, most of the higher end Napa cabs from 1998 have shown well.)

Agreed. I served the 1998 Araujo and the 1998 Opus (yeah, I know, overpriced and overhyped but still a good wine) against the 1999 Arrowood Reserve, all blind, to 11 experienced amateurs and the vote was 6 Opus, 5 Araujo and 0 Arrowood. I did not vote, or it would have been 6/6/0.
